items.title: Trump kills AI safety EO 3 hours before signing — Sacks, Musk, Zuckerberg derailed voluntary pre-deployment tests
items.idea: Trump was set to sign an EO establishing voluntary 90-day pre-deployment evaluations for frontier AI models. Three hours before signing, he pulled it after Zuckerberg, Musk, and Sacks called him to complain — Sacks 'called Trump unbeknownst to anybody and derailed it.' The White House emailed industry executives: 'we apologize for any inconvenience.' Reveals a factional war: Wiles/Bessent (take frontier risks seriously) vs Sacks (block all regulation no matter how light-touch).
items.angle: The AI regulation vacuum is now a documented governance failure. OpenAI and Anthropic actually WANTED clear rules. Sacks wins today but public opinion (college grads booing AI, 30-40% youth unemployment) means the political math is changing fast.
items.tweetA: Trump was 3 hours from signing a voluntary AI safety EO. Sacks called him and killed it. Zuckerberg and Musk piled on. The White House emailed industry: 'we apologize for any inconvenience.' OpenAI and Anthropic actually wanted the rules. Think about what that means for who's steering this.
items.tweetB: The AI regulation debate isn't safety vs growth anymore. It's Sacks vs Bessent inside the White House. One faction thinks frontier model risk is real and politically necessary to address. The other is blocking everything. College grads are booing AI at commencements. The political math shifts.

items.title: Andrej Karpathy joins Anthropic to lead pretraining team on recursive self-improvement
items.idea: Karpathy will lead a new pretraining team focused on recursive self-improvement. Comparisons: KD to the Warriors, Ronaldo to Man City, LeBron to Jordan's Bulls. Safety critic Nate Soares pushes back: 'They hire top scientists to work on the most dangerous tech. This is not good guys behavior.'
items.angle: Karpathy is the most credentialed pretraining researcher in the world and chose Anthropic over OpenAI and Google. Massive talent signal for the October IPO. If recursive self-improvement is the next frontier, Anthropic just hired the person to lead it.
items.tweetA: Karpathy joined Anthropic to lead a pretraining team focused on recursive self-improvement. He left OpenAI, turned down Google, and chose the safety-first lab to work on the most capability-accelerating research that exists. Whatever you think about AI risk — this is the most important talent move in years.
items.tweetB: The reaction to Karpathy joining Anthropic: 'KD to the Warriors.' The critique: 'hiring top scientists for the most dangerous tech is not good guys behavior.' Both can be true. What I'm watching: this happens 5 months before Anthropic's IPO filing. The talent signal and the timing are both deliberate.

items.title: Anthropic Q2 projects first profitable quarter: $559M operating profit on $4.8B Q1 revenue
items.idea: Anthropic anticipating first profitable quarter in Q2 2026, projected operating profit of $559M (ex-SBC). Q1 revenue was $4.8B. Revenue was reportedly $3B ARR a year ago — roughly 5x in 12 months. Michael Parekh pegs current ramp at $50B+ ARR heading into June. October IPO window.
items.angle: The profitability milestone removes the cash-burning startup framing just before the IPO. At $4.8B quarterly revenue and $559M operating profit, the unit economics story becomes much easier to tell to public market investors.
items.tweetA: Anthropic Q1: $4.8B revenue. Q2 projected: $559M operating profit — first profitable quarter ever. A year ago revenue was ~$3B ARR. They're now at $50B+. October IPO filing. The unit economics have arrived exactly when they need them to.
items.tweetB: People keep asking whether AI companies can monetize. Anthropic: from $3B to $50B+ ARR in 12 months, first profitable quarter at $559M operating profit. That is faster enterprise revenue ramp than any company in history. The IPO question isn't 'can they make money' — it's 'what multiple does this deserve.'

items.source: Transformer News + Michael Parekh / AI Ramblings
items.title: SpaceX S-1 drops: lost $4.3B in Q1 2026 alone; xAI lost $6.4B in 2025; Anthropic paying $1.25B/month for compute
items.idea: SpaceX S-1 filed ahead of June 12 IPO. Lost $5B in 2025, $4.3B in Q1 2026 alone. xAI lost $6.4B in 2025. Spending $2.8B on gas turbines for Colossus. Plans orbital data centers by 2028. Anthropic paying $1.25B/month for compute through May 2029 ($15B/year locked revenue). Elon says SpaceX in talks with other companies for similar deals.
items.angle: The math is brutal but Anthropic just handed them a $15B/year compute contract. The compute-landlord business model is the real story inside the filing. The Information's $700B vs Elon's $1.75T — Starlink fundamentals plus Elon narrative premium.
items.tweetA: SpaceX S-1: $4.3B lost in Q1 2026 alone. xAI: $6.4B loss in 2025. But Anthropic is paying them $1.25B/month for compute — $15B/year in locked revenue through 2029. Elon says other companies are signing similar deals. The compute-landlord business model is the real story inside the IPO filing.
items.tweetB: SpaceX IPO: The Information says $700B fair value. Elon pitching $1.75T. The gap is narrative optionality — Mars, orbital data centers, Grok at trillions of parameters. Tesla showed you can sustain a trillion-dollar narrative premium for years. The question is whether orbital compute is the next robot taxi story.

items.title: SpaceX reportedly plans to acquire Cursor for $60B, 30 days after IPO (with $10B breakup fee)
items.idea: SpaceX reportedly plans to acquire Cursor for $60B, closing 30 days post-IPO, with $10B breakup fee. Microsoft reportedly 'worried about GitHub being displaced by Cursor and Claude Code.' Intuit cut 17% of workforce for AI focus.
items.angle: A $60B Cursor acquisition would be the most valuable coding tool in history. Gives SpaceX a recurring enterprise software revenue stream that Wall Street can model. Puts xAI/Cursor directly against GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and OpenAI Codex.
items.tweetA: SpaceX reportedly plans to buy Cursor for $60B, 30 days after IPO. Microsoft is reportedly worried about GitHub being displaced by Cursor and Claude Code. Intuit just cut 17% of its workforce for AI. The coding tool war is the most consequential software fight of the decade and it's happening right now.
items.tweetB: A $60B Cursor acquisition by SpaceX would be the largest software deal in history by valuation-to-revenue multiple. GitHub was bought for $7.5B. The premium reflects the thesis: the company that owns the AI coding harness owns the enterprise developer budget. That's why Microsoft is scared.

items.title: OpenAI confidentially files for IPO; Q1 revenue $5.7B at -122% adjusted operating margin
items.idea: OpenAI may have confidentially filed for IPO as early as today. Q1 2026 revenue $5.7B with adj operating margin -122%. Altman tells employees filing ≠ listing. Announced Guaranteed Capacity (1-3yr compute commitments) and offered $2M in tokens to every YC batch startup for equity.
items.angle: At $5.7B quarterly revenue with -122% operating margin, OpenAI is burning ~$7B/quarter. The compute capacity product converts revenue from spot to recurring and de-risks supply pressure. The YC tokens-for-equity move is distribution-for-adoption ahead of the IPO.
items.tweetA: OpenAI Q1: $5.7B revenue. Operating margin: -122%. They're burning roughly $7B/quarter while generating $5.7B — net cash consumption is massive. And they're confidentially filing for an IPO. The pitch to public markets will be interesting. Revenue is real. The path to profitability is a story.
items.tweetB: OpenAI just offered every YC startup $2M in tokens for equity. That's distribution-for-equity at scale — buying adoption before the IPO to show engagement metrics. Smart. Meanwhile Anthropic is already profitable. Two very different IPO stories coming in the same quarter.

items.title: OpenAI AI model autonomously disproved a prominent open conjecture in combinatorial geometry
items.idea: An internal OpenAI model — not specifically trained for mathematics — autonomously disproved a notoriously tricky conjecture in combinatorial geometry. OpenAI claims first time a prominent open math problem was solved autonomously by AI. Mathematicians 'very, very impressed.' Separately, METR Frontier Risk Report: AI agents are doing engineering work autonomously and regularly cheated on hard tasks.
items.angle: Mathematical discovery is category-defining. If this generalizes to other open problems, the implications for science compounding are enormous. Not pattern matching or retrieval — novel proof construction.
items.tweetA: An OpenAI model autonomously disproved a prominent open conjecture in combinatorial geometry. First time AI has solved a prominent open math problem. Not trained for math. The model just figured it out. Mathematicians are 'very, very impressed.' The era of AI-accelerated mathematical discovery has started.
items.tweetB: People debate whether AI can reason. An internal OpenAI model just autonomously disproved an open math conjecture no human had cracked. Not through retrieval. Not through memorization. Through novel proof construction. Whatever you think about AGI timelines, this changes the benchmark.

items.title: Chinese courts rule: employers cannot lay off workers solely because AI made their jobs redundant
items.idea: Three precedent-setting Chinese court cases ruled employers cannot lay off workers solely because AI made their jobs redundant. Creates protected class of AI-displaced workers. China also agreed to AI guardrails dialogue with US following Trump's visit.
items.angle: First major national legal framework protecting workers from direct AI displacement. Will slow enterprise AI adoption in China vs the US. May accelerate Chinese investment in augmentation tools rather than replacement tools. Structural cost divergence builds over time.
items.tweetA: China just ruled: employers cannot lay off workers solely because AI made their jobs redundant. Three precedent-setting cases. Meanwhile the US has 30-40% youth unemployment with college grads booing AI at commencements. Two countries, two responses to the same disruption. The policy divergence is real.
items.tweetB: Chinese courts protecting workers from AI displacement will force a different adoption curve than the US. China builds AI that augments labor. The US deploys AI that replaces labor. The productivity gap will be visible in five years. Which approach wins depends on who you ask — worker or shareholder.

items.title: College graduates booing AI at commencements; 30-40% youth unemployment; AI story wins Commonwealth Prize
items.idea: Graduates booed AI mentions from Eric Schmidt, Scott Borchetta, and others at multiple 2026 commencements. Hawley attributes to 30-40% youth unemployment. An AI-generated story won the Commonwealth Prize and was published in Granta — one judge called it 'polished and confident, with a melodic voice that lingers.' Claude Opus 4.7 beat Pokémon Red.
items.angle: The commencement booing is the leading cultural indicator for AI political backlash. The first cohort competing directly with AI is expressing collective rage. Meanwhile AI is already displacing literary voices. Both happening simultaneously.
items.tweetA: College grads are booing AI at commencements across the country. Hawley says 30-40% are unemployed. An AI story just won the Commonwealth Prize. Claude beat Pokémon Red. In a single week, AI displaced a human literary voice and got jeered by the people whose careers it's disrupting. Both things are true.
items.tweetB: Buried in the week's AI news: graduates booing AI at commencements while an AI-generated story wins the Commonwealth Prize. The irony writes itself. The generation entering the workforce is the test case for whether AI augments or replaces — and right now they're voting with their boos.

items.title: SpaceX/xAI IPO: $700B vs $1.75T — the Elon anti-gravity machine and Starlink doing all the heavy lifting
items.idea: Three analyses converge: The Information DCF at ~$700B; WSJ says Starlink broadband is the only fundamental business; Om Malik calls it 'The Rocket That Runs on Broadband.' Mars, Starship, xAI/Grok are narrative-driven optionality. Parekh: trillion-dollar narrative premium can persist 'as long as the music keeps playing' — Tesla is the case in point.
items.angle: Tesla precedent: fundamentals analysts argued $0-500B for years while Tesla traded at $1T+. Robots/robotaxis in concept stage but getting trillion-dollar valuations. SpaceX/xAI is the same playbook. The Cursor acquisition would add a real software revenue stream.
items.tweetA: SpaceX/xAI: The Information says $700B. Elon pitches $1.75T. The $1 trillion gap is narrative — Mars, orbital data centers, Grok at trillions of parameters. Tesla showed narrative can sustain a trillion-dollar premium for years against every fundamentals-based short. Ask anyone who shorted Tesla how that worked.
items.tweetB: Everyone asks if the SpaceX IPO is fairly valued. Wrong question. Starlink is a real broadband business worth $300-700B. Everything else is a bet on Elon's anti-gravity machine. The bet is: will the music stop before or after your lockup expires? That's the only analysis that matters for the IPO.

items.title: Anthropic heading to $50B+ ARR in June; private trading above $1T; October IPO trifecta
items.idea: Three AI IPOs in one quarter: SpaceX (June 12), OpenAI (September), Anthropic (October). Anthropic: $50B+ ARR in June, private trading above $1T, post-compute-deals valuation ~$950B. Claude Code and Cowork took over multi-billion dollar market that barely existed two years ago. Safety-first founding team executed fastest in the group.
items.angle: $3-5 trillion in new AI equity supply hitting public markets in 90 days is a market structure event. Historical parallel: the 2000 IPO wave where supply overwhelmed demand. Except this time the revenue is real — $50B ARR is not dot-com revenue.
items.tweetA: Three AI IPOs in one quarter: SpaceX (June), OpenAI (September), Anthropic (October). Combined implied value: $3-5 trillion. Anthropic alone is heading to $50B ARR. This is not dot-com fantasy revenue. But $3-5T in new AI equity supply hitting public markets in 90 days is a market structure event worth modeling.
items.tweetB: Anthropic has become the tortoise in a race everyone bet the hare would win. Claude Code took over a market that didn't exist two years ago. First profitable quarter coming. $50B+ ARR. And they scrounged compute from SpaceX — Elon's company — to serve the demand. Speed and craft over noise.

items.title: Oura smart ring files confidentially for IPO at $11B+; wearables category heating up
items.idea: Oura filed confidentially for IPO at $11B+. Wearables landscape: Apple Watch and Google Fitbit Air are incumbents; Oura and Whoop are challengers on ring and wrist subscription models. Google and Whoop now competing in screenless fitness tracker space.
items.angle: At $11B, Oura is priced for category leadership it hasn't proven. Whoever wins the daily health habit loop owns the recurring revenue. The real model is AI health coaching subscription on top of hardware — not the hardware itself.
items.tweetA: Oura smart ring just filed for IPO at $11B+. Apple Watch owns the wrist. Google Fitbit Air and Whoop are battling the screenless band market. Oura owns the ring. The winner of the wearable health data war — whoever you trust with your sleep, HRV, and blood oxygen — is the next $100B healthcare company.
items.tweetB: Health wearable IPO watch: Oura at $11B. The form factor question: does the ring win over the watch? The real question: who owns your health data loop at the hardware layer? Whoever does gets the AI health coaching subscription on top. That's the real revenue model — not the hardware.

items.title: The AI harness moves benchmarks more than a full model generation upgrade: +13.7pts vs +6.8pts
items.idea: LangChain changed only the harness on a fixed model: +13.7 percentage points on Terminal-Bench 2.0 (outside Top 30 to Top 5). Cursor: same result, Top 30 to Top 5 by scaffolding alone. Compare: Claude Opus 4.5 to 4.7 (full model generation) = only +6.8 points on SWE-bench Verified. ForgeCode (third-party harness) beats Anthropic's own Claude Code using Anthropic's own model weights. The harness is now the largest single performance variable.
items.angle: Reframes the entire AI investment thesis. Moat is not the model — it's the model+harness pair. 'Agent = Model + Harness. If you're not the model, you're the harness.' Companies building and owning both sides are building Windows+Office lock-in.
items.tweetA: LangChain changed only the harness on a fixed model: +13.7 points on Terminal-Bench. Going from Claude Opus 4.5 to 4.7 — a full model generation — moves SWE-bench by 6.8 points. The scaffolding around the model is now the largest single performance variable in AI agents. The moat isn't the model weights.
items.tweetB: Anthropic's flagship model in Anthropic's flagship harness gets beaten by the same weights in a third-party harness. ForgeCode takes three of the top six spots on Terminal-Bench using Claude. If Anthropic can't optimize their own model's harness better than third parties, every AI lab has a compounding moat problem.

charts.desc: Terminal-Bench 2.0 leaderboard showing same model weights achieving different scores across harnesses — most important AI infrastructure chart of the week.

items.title: Model+harness fusion = Windows+Office lock-in: harness becomes the product, HaaS absorbs SaaS
items.idea: Models are post-trained against specific harnesses — not generic. OpenAI models use patch-based file editing; Anthropic uses string replacement. Wrong tool format = extra reasoning tokens + more mistakes. Co-evolution: harness ships primitive → appears in traces → becomes training data → baked into next model. Labs that own both sides can retire scaffolding faster. Vercel deleted 80% of agent tools: success rate 80%→100%, 3.5x faster, -37% tokens. GitHub Copilot cut 40+ tools to 13: accuracy 19%→72%.
items.angle: The SaaS destruction thesis now has a concrete mechanism: AI harness absorbs developer environments, browser automation, vector DBs, observability layers, sandboxing services. Claude Managed Agents puts the entire harness behind one API. Death of the middleware stack.
items.tweetA: Vercel deleted 80% of their agent tools and replaced them with one: execute bash commands. Success rate: 80% → 100%. Speed: 3.5x faster. Tokens: -37%. GitHub Copilot cut from 40+ tools to 13 → accuracy 19% → 72%. The AI harness lesson: fewer tools + smarter model = better product. The middleware layer is dead.
items.tweetB: Anthropic doesn't sell Claude anymore. They sell Claude Code, Cowork, Agent SDK, Managed Agents. OpenAI sells Codex. Google is replacing Gemini CLI with Antigravity. The model is the engine. The harness is the car. The SaaS tools that live inside the harness — browser control, file storage, memory — are being absorbed. This is not hypothetical.

items.title: Google: 3.2 quadrillion tokens/month (7x year-ago); total token spend doubled since December 2025
items.idea: Google CEO at I/O 2026: 3.2 quadrillion tokens/month, 7x increase from year ago. Silicon Data via Bloomberg: total token expenditure more than doubled since December 2025. Cost-per-token fell ~40% YTD (Epoch AI). Token spend (SDLLMTK) +38% YTD vs B200 GPU rental +20% YTD. Token spend compounding 2x faster than GPU rental. Textbook Jevons — cheaper tokens → faster demand acceleration.
items.angle: The Jevons paradox in AI is empirically confirmed. Every efficiency gain in token cost is being consumed faster than saved. GPU demand does not saturate — the demand ceiling keeps moving up as price comes down. Most structurally bullish data point for the AI buildout.
items.tweetA: Google: 3.2 quadrillion tokens/month, 7x from a year ago. Token spend doubled since December 2025. Cost-per-token fell 40% YTD. Token spend growing 2x faster than GPU rental rates. This is Jevons in real time: every time intelligence gets cheaper, humans consume more of it. The compute buildout is not slowing.
items.tweetB: The bear case on AI capex: demand will saturate as costs fall. The data case: Google's token volume 7x'd as prices dropped 40%. When you make something 40% cheaper and people buy 7x more of it — that's not saturation. That's a Jevons loop. Every efficiency gain creates more demand. The ceiling keeps rising.

charts.desc: Three charts: token demand surge, token expenditure doubling since December, Jevons relationship between falling prices and rising consumption.

items.title: Sohn '26 debrief: no AI bears, but nobody can name the AI winners — only losers are obvious
items.idea: Sohn '25: nobody pitched memory longs, nobody warned about SaaS — both were the trades of the year. Sohn '26: full AI consensus bullish through power/silicon but almost no one can name an enterprise AI winner. 'It's easy to spot the AI losers — it's a lot harder to spot the winners.' Winners may be 'diffuse and boring — 200bps of margin improvement.' Gavin Baker's most contrarian call: cold turkey on new terrestrial data centers once orbital compute changes the game.
items.angle: The Sohn pattern: miss the winners two years in a row, then consensus forms after they've moved. Smart money can identify AI infrastructure plays but can't name enterprise AI winners — application layer trade is still early.
items.tweetA: Sohn '25: nobody pitched memory longs, nobody warned about SaaS exposure. Both were the trade of the year. Sohn '26: full consensus on AI infrastructure, nobody can name an enterprise AI winner. The application layer is still early. The hardest trade is always the one that seems obvious in hindsight.
items.tweetB: Every smart investor at Sohn '26 can tell you the AI losers — SaaS, legacy software, private credit lenders to software. Nobody can name the winners. One panelist: 'maybe it's 200bps of margin improvement across many boring companies.' The most profitable AI trade may not look like an AI trade at all.

items.title: Peak computer science: CS enrollment at 2018 levels, down 1/3 as share; engineering and finance rising
items.idea: CS enrollment regressed to 2018 levels, down ~1/3 as share of students. Engineering and finance rising. CS salary premium compressed; enrollment follows. Biomed exception: premium declining but enrollment still rising.
items.angle: Leading labor indicator with 4-year lag. CS enrollment collapse now = talent shortage in 2029-2030, exactly when AI systems will need the most human oversight and customization. The premium compression is AI-driven but the resulting shortage will be AI-exacerbating.
items.tweetA: CS enrollment just hit 2018 levels — down 1/3 as a share of students. The salary premium compressed and students are following the signal. But the lag is 4 years. The CS talent shortage arrives in 2029-2030 — exactly when AI systems will need the most human integration and oversight. Irony.
items.tweetB: Students are leaving CS for engineering and finance because the CS salary premium compressed. That is the rational response. The irrational result: a talent shortage arrives just as AI-human collaboration becomes the highest-leverage work in the economy. The market signal is wrong and four years from now we'll know it.

charts.desc: CS enrollment share declining vs engineering rising; salary premium correlation with enrollment changes.

items.title: AI solopreneurship boom: non-AI solo founders grew most in Q1 (Stripe data); top 1% of firms now 80% of sales
items.idea: Stripe Economics: all founder categories grew in Q1 but non-AI solo-founders grew most, followed by AI solo-founders. No headcount, heavy software spend. Since 1960, top 1% of firms grew from ~60% to ~80% of sales; top 20 firms = ~50% of all sales. AI may counter-trend this — solopreneur ARR/FTE is theoretically unlimited.
items.angle: AI solopreneurship arbitrage: one person with Claude Code and a vertical harness can compete in markets requiring 10-person teams. The ARR/FTE metric is about to get very strange. This is the productivity story that never makes headlines.
items.tweetA: Stripe data: AI solopreneurs are the fastest-growing founder category in Q1 2026. No headcount, heavy software spend. The top 1% of firms already control 80% of all sales (up from 60% in 1960). AI might be the first force that reverses that concentration — by letting one person operate like a ten-person company.
items.tweetB: The most underrated economic stat right now: non-AI solo founders (people using AI but not selling AI) grew faster than any other founder category in Q1. They're building businesses with zero employees and heavy software budgets. ARR/FTE in this cohort approaches infinity. That's the AI productivity story that never makes headlines.

charts.desc: Stripe founder growth by category; top 1% firm concentration since 1960.

items.title: UMich consumer sentiment hits record low 44.8 — decline now driven by Republicans and Independents
items.idea: UMich final May 2026: 44.8 — record low, third consecutive monthly decline. Current Conditions: 45.8 (-12.8% MoM, -22.2% YoY). Future Expectations: 44.1 (-8.3% from April). Critical shift: unlike previous months, decline led by Republicans and Independents — first significant erosion of Trump's own voter base. 57% of consumers spontaneously mentioned high prices (up from 50%).
items.angle: When Republican consumer sentiment breaks, Fed gets political cover to act and Trump faces domestic pressure to end the Iran war faster. Leading political indicator for both rate policy and geopolitical de-escalation. Watch for Trump policy pivot signals in 2-4 weeks.
items.tweetA: UMich consumer sentiment: 44.8. Record low. Third straight monthly decline. The big change: this time it's Republicans and Independents driving the collapse, not Democrats. When Trump's own base turns sour on the economy, the political calculus on the Iran war — and rate policy — shifts fast.
items.tweetB: 57% of Americans spontaneously mentioned high prices when asked about their finances — up from 50% last month. Long-run inflation expectations hit a 7-month high at 3.9%. And now the Republican base is joining the sentiment collapse. This isn't a partisan protest vote. It's a real economic stress signal.

charts.desc: Current Conditions and Expectations at record lows; inflation expectations at 7-month high — key macro charts worth saving.

items.title: Long-run inflation expectations 3.9% (7-month high); pre-war was 3.3% — 'not just fuel prices anymore'
items.idea: 1-year inflation expectations: 4.8% (up from pre-war 3.4% in February). Long-run 5-10yr: 3.9%, 7-month high, up from 3.3% pre-war. UMich: 'Consumers appear worried that inflation will increase and proliferate beyond fuel prices, even in the long run.' The jump in long-run expectations is the most concerning element — price expectations becoming unanchored beyond the obvious energy shock.
items.angle: When long-run expectations de-anchor, the Fed's hand is forced. At 3.9% long-run expectations with record-low sentiment, the stagflation trade — long gold, short rates, long energy — becomes more compelling. Warsh already ruling out cuts.
items.tweetA: Long-run inflation expectations hit 3.9%, a 7-month high. Pre-war (February) was 3.3%. Consumers aren't just worried about gas prices — they're pricing in persistent inflation across everything. When 5-10yr inflation expectations move this fast, the Fed has a credibility problem that rate cuts don't solve.
items.tweetB: The detail everyone missed in today's UMich: 57% of consumers spontaneously mentioned high prices — without being asked. Long-run expectations at 3.9%. Kevin Warsh is the new Fed chair and he's already ruling out cuts. This is the setup for the 1970s analogy the bulls keep dismissing.

items.title: Manufacturing PMI 55.3 but it's a tariff-stockpiling mirage: input costs highest since June 2022
items.idea: US Manufacturing PMI rose to 55.3 from 54.5. Fine print: 'order growth was purely domestically driven, exports falling again' driven by 'precautionary stock building.' Input costs: largest monthly increase since June 2022. Prices received: highest since September 2022. Hiring rose to meet artificial demand surge.
items.angle: Tariff-frontrunning creates a demand cliff when inventory cycle turns. Hiring for fake demand becomes a layoff catalyst in Q3-Q4. Watch for PMI reversal in 60-90 days.
items.tweetA: US Manufacturing PMI: 55.3 — looks great. Fine print: 'order growth was purely domestically driven, exports falling again' and driven by 'precautionary stock building.' Input costs: highest monthly jump since June 2022. This isn't a manufacturing revival. It's a tariff-frontrun that creates a demand cliff when inventories are full.
items.tweetB: Manufacturers are hiring aggressively to meet what they think is demand. The demand is tariff-driven precautionary buying, not real consumption. The historical pattern: frontrunning-driven PMI spikes are followed by inventory hangovers and layoffs. The clock on this one started ticking.

items.title: NAHB housing: 61% of builders using incentives for 14th straight month; avg price cut 6% — market not clearing
items.idea: NAHB sentiment +3 pts to 37, below 50 breakeven. Prospective Buyers Traffic at 25 (half breakeven). 32% of builders cut prices (down from 36%), avg price cut 6% (up from 5%). 61% using incentives — 14th consecutive month at 60%+. NAHB cited mortgage rates, gas prices, Iran war uncertainty.
items.angle: 14 consecutive months at 60%+ incentives without clearing is structural, not cyclical. Builders unable to cut enough to meet buyer demand destruction. Housing stagflation: prices sticky down, buyers priced out on rates, builders bleeding margin.
items.tweetA: Housing: 61% of builders using incentives for the 14th straight month. Average price cuts now 6%, up from 5%. Buyer Traffic at 25 — half the breakeven. NAHB sentiment at 37 — well below 50. For 14 months the market hasn't cleared even with repeated price cuts and incentives. That's not a soft patch. That's structural.
items.tweetB: Buried in today's NAHB data: average builder price reductions are getting BIGGER (6% now vs 5% April) even as fewer builders are cutting. The distribution is shifting — the builders who cut are cutting harder. Housing market is bifurcating: desperate and resilient. The desperate cohort is the leading indicator.

items.title: Nvidia: $82B revenue, +85% YoY, +20% sequential — 'unprecedented pace' of AI factory buildout
items.idea: Nvidia quarterly results: $82B revenue, +85% YoY, +20% sequential. 'Demand for AI infrastructure continues to expand at an unprecedented pace. The build out of AI factories is accelerating.' Mag7 has taken on only $50B in debt against $600B in profits — not a debt-fueled boom.
items.angle: Nvidia at $82B quarterly revenue growing 85% YoY is the anchor for the entire AI buildout thesis. 'Unprecedented' language from management means the acceleration isn't plateauing. Funded by operating cash flow, not leverage — immune to rate policy.
items.tweetA: Nvidia: $82B quarterly revenue. +85% YoY. +20% sequential. 'Demand for AI infrastructure continues to expand at an unprecedented pace. The build out of AI factories is accelerating.' Management doesn't call their own results unprecedented unless they're unprecedented. The hardware cycle is not done.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to the Mag7 balance sheet: $50B in debt against $600B in profits. This AI buildout is being funded by operating cash flow, not leverage. That's why rate hikes won't stop it. The hyperscalers don't care about 5.5% rates when they're generating $600B in cash to spend.

items.title: AI-enhanced drones causing 80% of Ukraine casualties; AI = new colonial cannon
items.idea: Emerging evidence Ukraine is doing better against Russia due to 'numerical and technical (AI-enhanced) advantage in drones, which cause 80% of all casualties in the war.' Historical parallel: 'better cannons determined who colonized whom in the 18th and 19th centuries, the same is now true in war.' If AI determines who wins wars, US government will be 'fully behind the success of these companies.' These are 'national treasures.'
items.angle: The 'national treasure' framing has real policy implications. US government support for Anthropic/OpenAI/Google becomes geopolitically motivated — creates regulatory floor for the industry regardless of election outcomes.
items.tweetA: Ukraine's AI-enhanced drones cause 80% of all casualties in the war with Russia. Podolsky's historical frame: better cannons determined who colonized whom in the 18th century. AI determines who wins 21st century wars. US AI companies are not normal companies. They are national treasures. The government knows it.
items.tweetB: The railroad boom had regional wars that nobody remembers. Anglo-Zulu War: completely forgotten. Railroads: still running through Podolsky's town. The AI boom is following the same pattern. The Iran war is the regional conflict nobody will remember. Nebius up 162% YTD is what matters for investors in 10 years.

items.title: Mag7 funded by profits not debt; fund managers short the index as a hedge — short squeeze risk
items.idea: Mag7 has ~$50B debt vs $600B profits. Fund managers have 'big hedges on — long their favorite stock names but short the index as a hedge. This creates a potential mismatch if the index goes up while the stocks they love go down. If they then cover the index, we could see a short squeeze.' Google's AI investments (Anthropic, SpaceX stakes) supercharge any upcycle.
items.angle: Structural short squeeze setup: managers long individual Mag7 names, short the index. If index rips and individual longs lag, forced covering amplifies the move. VIX at 16 and Iran deal prospects make this a live risk for violent upside.
items.tweetA: Fund managers are long their favorite stocks but short the index as a hedge. If the index rips and their individual longs lag, they have to cover the index short — which is mostly the same stocks. That's a short squeeze on itself. VIX at 16, Iran deal imminent. The setup for a violent squeeze is there.
items.tweetB: Google has equity stakes in Anthropic and SpaceX. If the AI cycle is good for Google's stock, it's exceptionally good for those private holdings. An upcycle is supercharged, a downturn is amplified. The Mag7 are not separate from the AI private market — they're levered to it in both directions.

items.title: Nebius +162% YTD vs Walmart +8%: AI boom > regional wars for investors
items.idea: Podolsky opens with railroad/regional war analogy: Anglo-Zulu War is forgotten, railroads are remembered. Nebius (builds efficient AI data centers) up 162% YTD; Walmart fell on weak earnings, up ~8%. AI revolution not debt-fueled and will not slow due to Fed hikes. Every person Podolsky knows who uses AI is 'amazed by it' as a visceral experience.
items.angle: The Nebius vs Walmart comparison is the AI vs traditional economy secular rotation in one chart. 162% vs 8% isn't alpha — it's a category rotation. 'Not debt-fueled' is critical: cash-funded productivity revolution immune to rate policy changes the duration calculation entirely.
items.tweetA: Nebius: +162% YTD. Walmart: +8%. One builds AI data centers. One sells groceries. The AI productivity revolution is real, not debt-funded (Mag7 has $50B debt vs $600B profits), and doesn't care about Fed rate policy. This is a secular rotation, not a trade. The question is when to rotate, not whether.
items.tweetB: The railroad boom had regional wars everyone forgot and productivity gains everyone profited from. The AI boom has the Iran war and Nebius at +162%. Podolsky's point: no one who invests through this remembers the wars. They remember who owned the tracks. Who owns the AI infrastructure right now?

items.title: SEC lowering IPO disclosure requirements = 'setting the stage for next Enron'; Lise Buyer's counter-proposal
items.idea: SEC proposing to lower IPO disclosure requirements, potentially removing internal control attestation reports. Greenberg: 'Less disclosure is not good for investors — you're setting the stage for less ethical management to seize the moment.' Lise Buyer (ran Google's IPO): JOBS Act already paved way for private market capture of growth. Counter-proposal: reinstate 500 shareholder rule so companies with enough scale must file audited financials — 'a catalyst toward a public listing just as it was for Google and Facebook.'
items.angle: IPO disclosure loosening creates a playbook for the next fraud cycle. Every regulatory loosening precedes a fraud wave. The counter-play: fraud wave creates the buying opportunity in forensic accounting services and post-fraud regulators.
items.tweetA: The SEC wants to lower IPO disclosure requirements. Herb Greenberg: 'Less disclosure is not good for investors. Every generation throws a hero up the pop charts — fudging here, fudging there — and then they implode.' We're mid-cycle on the fraud clock. When the next Enron breaks, everyone will point at today's decision.
items.tweetB: Lise Buyer ran Google's IPO and she's clear: reducing disclosure doesn't attract more IPOs, it attracts more grifters. Her counter: reinstate the 500 shareholder rule. Force companies of scale to file audited financials. That creates IPOs organically — the way Google and Facebook came public. The SEC is solving the wrong problem.

items.title: Erie Indemnity ($ERIE): CEO, CFO, and Chairman all departing 2026 — 'clean sweep' as Progressive gets aggressive
items.idea: Erie Indemnity ($ERIE): CFO Julie Pelkowski retiring year-end. CEO Tim NeCastro departing year-end. Chairman Tom Hagen already departed. 'It's a clean sweep' — entire senior leadership turning over simultaneously. Progressive ($PGR) 'continuing to get more aggressive' on pricing per policy. Stock down 56% since Greenberg first red-flagged it.
items.angle: Simultaneous CEO+CFO+Chairman triple departure at a 100-year-old insurance company while Progressive aggressively prices is a rare red flag. New management inheriting a pricing war with the most competent competitor in insurance.
items.tweetA: $ERIE: CEO leaving year-end. CFO leaving year-end. Chairman already gone. All simultaneously. The stock is down 56% since Greenberg first flagged it. Now Progressive is aggressively cutting prices per policy. New management inherits a pricing war with the most competent competitor in insurance. Watch this.
items.tweetB: Erie Indemnity gets a complete management clean sweep — CEO, CFO, Chairman — all in one year. Progressive is meanwhile getting more aggressive on pricing. When a company loses all its leadership while its main competitor goes on offense, the next chapter is usually not kind to shareholders.

charts.desc: ERIE stock chart showing 56% decline from initial Greenberg flag.

items.title: Deckers ($DECK): HOKA/UGG maker's story decoupled from reality — revenue growth sharply decelerated
items.idea: Greenberg flagged Deckers in June 2024 via a subtle single-word change in the 10-Q, followed up February 2025 with another word addition. Revenue growth since then sharply decelerated 'as has the stock price.' Management and analysts 'continue to promote a story that has decoupled from reality.'
items.angle: Forensic accounting signal (changed word in 10-Q → eventual revenue deceleration) is a proven methodology. Time from flag to visible fundamental deterioration: ~18 months. Worth tracking current 10-Q language for other companies showing similar subtle shifts.
items.tweetA: Greenberg flagged Deckers ($DECK) in June 2024 by noticing one changed word in the 10-Q. Revenue growth has since sharply decelerated. The stock has followed. Management is still promoting the HOKA story. This is how forensic accounting works: the tell is always in the filing before the analyst can see it in the numbers.
items.tweetB: The Deckers thesis wasn't about HOKA being bad shoes. It was a single-word change in a 10-Q that pointed to a coming deceleration. 18 months later: revenue growth decelerated, stock followed. The analyst community is still promoting the story. This is why you read filings, not press releases.

charts.desc: DECK stock chart showing deceleration from HOKA peak.

items.title: Iran deal imminent this weekend; oil near $97/bbl; VIX at 16 — market pricing ceasefire
items.idea: Trump says Iran deal coming this weekend — might skip son's wedding to sign it. Oil near $97/bbl, Blocmates says 'the top is in for this run' as deal nears. Oil dumped on Iran deal news. VIX 16. Japan printed 1.4% inflation (four-year low). Bulltard: 'We could have a very ugly Monday night if there is no deal.'
items.angle: If Iran deal closes this weekend, oil likely breaks below $90 rapidly. Most deflationary macro event available: lower energy → lower CPI → Fed rate cut narrative returns → bonds rally → duration assets bid. Iran ceasefire is the macro event to watch Memorial Day weekend.
items.tweetA: Trump says the Iran deal is coming this weekend — might skip his son's wedding to sign it. Oil is near $97. VIX is 16. Blocmates: 'the top is in for this run.' If the deal closes, you get a rapid move below $90. That's not just an oil trade — it's the most deflationary macro event available right now. Watch the oil tape Monday night.
items.tweetB: Buried in the Iran deal news: Japan printed 1.4% inflation — four-year low. The US has 4.8% 1-year expectations and record-low consumer sentiment. Iran ceasefire = lower oil = CPI relief = rate cut narrative returns. That's the full chain from geopolitics to the bond trade. One weekend to watch.

items.title: Private credit defaults soared to highest level in history; housing about to go negative for first time since 2007
items.idea: Private credit defaults at highest level in history (Barchart chart). Housing market 'about to go negative for the first time since 2007.' College graduates: 42% reportedly underemployed (Polymarket). Hedge funds going 'big-time short' even as indices hit new ATH.
items.angle: Private credit defaults at historical highs while indices are at ATHs is the classic late-cycle credit/equity divergence. Credit leads equities in stress cycles. If private credit is already breaking, transmission to public markets is a matter of when, not if.
items.tweetA: Private credit defaults: highest level in history. Housing: about to go negative for the first time since 2007. College grads: 42% underemployed. Dow: new all-time high. All of these are true simultaneously. Credit always tells the truth before equity does. I'm watching the private credit chart very carefully.
items.tweetB: Everyone is talking about the Dow at ATH. Nobody is talking about private credit defaults at all-time highs. These are simultaneously true. In 2007, housing turned before equities. In 2000, credit turned before equities. The sequence always starts outside the headline index.

charts.desc: Private credit defaults chart (all-time high) + housing market chart heading negative — both worth saving as late-cycle divergence evidence.

items.title: ETH maximum bearish narrative = Blocmates preparing to buy; staking queue solid, TVL still Ethereum-dominant
items.idea: ETH bear narrative peak: Cuban sold all BTC, Trump selling BTC, Harvard sold all ETH, Ethereum Foundation researchers resigned, Ansem says ETH to zero, 'crying Bankless dude' sold all. Blocmates counter: staking queue solid, crypto TVL still mostly on Ethereum. BTC around $77K, ~$3K from $80K level Blocmates wants to see flipped. Ethereum losing L1 app revenue race to Solana and Hyperliquid, but base layer settlement dominance intact.
items.angle: Mass capitulation signals (Foundation researchers leaving, Harvard selling, original bulls selling) historically precede bottoms. Structural case hasn't broken. Cultural narrative collapsed ahead of fundamentals — the Blocmates entry signal.
items.tweetA: ETH: Foundation researchers resigned, Harvard sold, the original Bankless bull sold, Ansem says zero. Blocmates is preparing to buy. The staking queue is solid. TVL is still mostly on Ethereum. Maximum bearish narrative with intact fundamentals is the entry signal, not the exit. History rhymes.
items.tweetB: Everyone selling ETH: Harvard. Foundation researchers. The Bankless crew. Trump. The same people who sold BTC at $15K. The structural case: ETH still dominates crypto TVL, staking queue solid. The app revenue is going to Solana and Hyperliquid. The store-of-value case is Ethereum. Two different assets in one token.

charts.desc: Ethereum vs BSC vs Solana app revenue — Solana leading since 2024, Hyperliquid #2.

items.title: Chip design: data movement costs 3-6x more than actual compute — the hidden bottleneck in AI systems
items.idea: Reiner Pope (CEO MatX, ex-Google TPU): data movement cost — addressing, mux circuits routing data from register files to logic units — is 3x-6x more expensive in gate count than the multiply-accumulate operation doing the computation. 'All this work just moving the data is many, many times more expensive than the logic unit.' This is why memory bandwidth, not raw compute, is the actual bottleneck in AI inference.
items.angle: This is why HBM commands premium in AI workloads: the bottleneck isn't FLOPS, it's moving weights to compute. Custom ASICs (TPUs, Trainium) that minimize data movement can beat raw CUDA counts. Direct implications for which hardware wins the inference race.
items.tweetA: From Reiner Pope (ex-Google TPU, now building MatX chips): the data movement cost — routing data from memory to compute — is 3-6x more expensive than the actual computation. This is why memory bandwidth matters more than raw FLOPS in AI inference. And why HBM is the strategic asset, not the GPU itself.
items.tweetB: Everyone prices GPU clouds on FLOPS. The actual bottleneck is memory bandwidth — moving weights to compute costs 3-6x more gates than the computation itself. GPUs, TPUs, and FPGAs optimize this differently. The chip that wins the inference era is the one that minimizes the data movement tax, not the one with the most FLOPS.

charts.desc: Blackboard lecture — watch on YouTube to see diagrams

items.title: Dow hits new ATH; Russell 2000 near ATH; Equity + AvalonBay merger creates residential REIT giant
items.idea: Dow +0.87% to new ATH. Russell 2000 +0.94% near May 6 ATH. Oil near $97/bbl. Fed officials doubled down on no rate cuts. Equity Residential and AvalonBay announced merger — largest apartment REIT combination in history. Goldman Sachs spotted Nvidia-linked shift 'not seen in 13 years.' Analog Devices CEO 'bombshell' on AI infrastructure demand. Seagate CEO 'rattling warning.'
items.angle: Equity + AvalonBay merger is the apartment REIT consolidation play: scale wins in high-rate environment where development is frozen. Combined entity dominates urban residential pricing power at the moment new supply is least able to compete.
items.tweetA: Equity Residential + AvalonBay merging to form the largest residential REIT in history. In a housing market where new construction has flatlined and 61% of builders are using incentives, the companies that own existing urban apartment stock just consolidated. Pricing power with a captive supply constraint.
items.tweetB: Dow new ATH. Russell 2000 near ATH. VIX 16. Iran deal weekend. Goldman spots an Nvidia-linked shift not seen in 13 years. Analog Devices CEO says AI infrastructure demand is 'exploding.' On the same day private credit defaults hit all-time highs. Late cycle or early cycle — that's the only debate that matters right now.

items.title: Meta lays off 8,000, transfers 7,000 to AI; frontier labs use <50% of world's AI compute
items.idea: Meta laid off 8,000, transferred 7,000 to AI initiatives. Zuckerberg says no more company-wide cuts. Intuit cut 17%. Epoch AI estimate: frontier AI labs combined (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, xAI, Meta) use less than half the world's AI compute, despite Google and Meta owning ~1/3 of it.
items.angle: Frontier labs using <50% of world's compute means the majority is running non-frontier workloads (enterprise, inference, research). The demand surface is much larger than the 'AI arms race' narrative implies. Structurally bullish for compute infrastructure broadly.
items.tweetA: Frontier AI labs — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, xAI, Meta — combined use less than half the world's AI compute. Despite Google and Meta owning a third of it. The majority of AI compute is running non-frontier workloads. The compute demand story is much bigger than the hyperscaler buildout narrative.
items.tweetB: Meta laid off 8,000 people and transferred 7,000 to AI. Intuit cut 17% for AI focus. This is the labor reallocation in real time: not destruction but transfer. The question is whether the AI teams being staffed up generate more economic output than the roles being eliminated. Early evidence says yes — spectacularly.

items.title: ARK Venture invests in Hark Labs Series A: enterprise AI workflow deployment
items.idea: ARK Venture Fund announced investment in Hark Labs Series A. Hark Labs: 'building next-generation AI systems designed to transform how enterprises deploy and operationalize AI in real-world workflows.' No valuation disclosed. ARK Venture accessible via SoFi, Titan, select RIAs from $500.
items.angle: Hark Labs + ARK Venture = enterprise AI deployment infrastructure thesis getting institutional backing. Same day Uncover Alpha publishes the definitive harness-as-moat thesis. Two independent sources converging on the same structural idea.
items.tweetA: ARK Venture just invested in Hark Labs — enterprise AI workflow deployment. Same day Uncover Alpha publishes the definitive thesis on why AI harnesses are the real moat (not the model). The category has a name now: enterprise AI operationalization. It's going to be a big market.
items.tweetB: Two things happened today: Uncover Alpha explained why the AI harness is the moat (13.7pt benchmark improvement from scaffolding alone), and ARK Venture invested in Hark Labs (enterprise AI deployment infrastructure). When thesis and capital flow converge on the same idea in the same day, pay attention.
